speaker: interviewer
text: With Mrs. Fela Abramowicz. What happened after the big hunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, even before it ended and everybody-- were lot of casualties and there was hunger and there was everything, everybody. Nobody knew where to go, what to do. And so the commander, Tevye Bielski, had a meeting, sort of a whole assembly. it's called a meeting, sort of. And he told us it'll be safer to divide. It was a big triad then-- to divide it in groups, and go back to the-- the old district, to the-- they called it the [NON-ENGLISH]. Means the territory where we used to be before. And then the smaller groups, because this is such a big group, can't exist and being chased. We had got a better chance if we divided into groups. So that what happened, so divided into groups. And then there was one group that decided to stay in the forests. So made a request, about 50 people, they want to stay in the forest because they know the forest from this Naliboki, have all the guides and they all had arms. And so the commander gave them permission to stay in the forest. And among those 50 people was me. I was, and my husband. So we didn't go to the-- back to the areas, the old areas. We stayed in the Naliboki forest through the end of the siege. And then they burned all the-- and we used-- they kept-- the Germans kept pursuing us, at one stage. They went into a village close to where we were standing. And they were burning the villages and taking the peasants to Germany. And the cattle start running, and then the people start running after the cattle. And they came almost to where we were. We heard the voices, so we had to quickly escape and went to another place. And the other place we went to, we found the fires burning and the butts of cigarettes still alight from the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these 50 people who stayed behind-- so I was among them and my husband. And we were playing-- only because those were the people who knew every tree of that forest. And we-- we were playing with the Germans, cat and mouse. They came in where we were, we came where they were before, and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow we escaped them. Nobody got hurt, but it was very close. And eventually the siege-- it went to the-- to the places which got burnt. There's a lot of things were still on the fields, like potatoes, and vegetables, and honey, and runaway chicken and pigs and things, you know, because they couldn't burn everything in-- as they're taking away the peasants.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we eventually the siege ended, and we started gathering all that stuff from the fields. We started getting potatoes and bringing in all the useful things, items from the burned-out houses. So, you know, the windows and the doors, and also the stoves, and-- and other things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for-- for a little while, you know, I got very ill because after all this-- after all this I got very ill. I had a weak stomach condition, I had big ulcer on my breast, and altogether, you know, I was very-- very sick. And I was walking with a stick, and very-- decimated, you know, and everything. But the doctor examined me. He said, can't hear the heartbeat of the baby, and he can't cure my breast ulcer because I was-- has no medication. And anyway, we'll see. But this time was peaceful. We had made some tents-- not tents. Yeah, tents from straw. We gathered straw. They were warm. We put some straw in the-- in the bottom, we covered with peasant's rags-- and that's how I-- rags-- and that's how I slept, with my husband, and one of those.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was warm. This was still end of-- it was early autumn. And everything was in the fields-- there had lots of honey and lots of-- we had chickens and we had-- they brought some vegetables from the thing, and it was quiet, you know? Quite a good life for this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And only thing that I wasn't well, but nothing could be done about it. One day when my husband went on a mission first thing in the morning, at 5 o'clock I woke up the doctor and saying that I can't stand it anymore. You've got to operate on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took off-- everybody was still asleep, apart from the watch. And there was a fire burning, so he took a bit of a box of a few what he had, scissors and things like that, that are primitive instruments. He boiled them in the-- in the fire, and he opened up my breast and took out all the-- what was in it, lots of ulcer, sort of pus, and things like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, if he only had a few-- at this time it was called-- it wasn't antibiotics, it was something else. Strepto-something, streptosist or something-- they call it, this little red tablet. I could save your life, and in other case, we'll just have to-- to wait and see. I'll do my best.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he just kept putting this huge pieces of gauze-- he had this purple liquid, he had something crystals he-- to sort of kind of antiseptic, and he put them in. And every day he pulled them out with a thing, and the milk kept coming because I was pregnant-- seven months pregnant, or eighth month.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the pus kept coming, and the milk kept coming and draining the pus because this was infected. So all this took place, and I was-- I didn't know what was going to happen to me. But otherwise, it was peace and quiet in the-- for the time being. We ate well, and there was warm weather, and we could wash ourselves and clean and everything. So this was the only problem I had. And then it kept-- you know, it kept going and going and going and the wound grew smaller and smaller and smaller. And the thing around it kept getting harder and harder and harder. So, you know, I wasn't expecting to survive it. But one day it sort of all disappeared. And the doctor said, you're very lucky, because this wasn't medicine, and this wasn't reason. This was just luck. I had a very strong immune system. I was young and nature was very strong under those condition. I was covered with scabs, and we found-- everybody used to carry something like the sulfur ointment. This was like-- somebody-- this was just as important as water. Because if you didn't have this, your whole body got thing-- got-- And then after, just before I went into labor, I had a-- I had an attack of shingles. So I was very, very sick woman. And I expected a very difficult birth. I was very young and very small, and very everything. And the doctor was looking after me very well, though. And then the triad came back from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the districts. And we came one more bigger triad, again. And my husband was assigned to look after the-- not to go on missions because I was pregnant, and he already-- once there was a porter killed, you know. He had arms, so everybody who had arms-- and my husband says I don't want to. I want to be in the armed forces, not in the-- not in the thing, not in the-- they said, you stay here where you are, or else. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he stayed and became in charge of all the supplies that came in. There were big-- and fast. It was a temporary base. And while this base was going on, they were building a permanent base, with zemilyankas proper. You know, everything here was tents and everything, but in proper zemilyankas and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband was-- wasn't going away. He was close to me, and-- because I was sick and everything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day I decided-- this was in November and I thought, that's my time. I didn't even know the date when I'm expected to have my baby. It's just roughly estimated. And they told me to go-- I-- I went to my husband and I said, I think I'm starting to have symptoms that-- I didn't know, didn't have any experience. Never had a baby before.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to the doctor. He said you better go to the permanent base. It's not yet-- all zemilyankas are not yet built, but you find the best you-- you want, and stay there, you know. And the doctor will look after you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to this one day. I went under a cart, and somebody took me into this zemilyanka. It was already built-- not all of them were built yet. It was just the beginning of the-- of the base, which was the last till the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a a very-- as it turned out, It was like a-- turned out into something else. A little genuine little town. What was-- in eight months what was built there, what was going on was just indescribable, unbelievable. Anyway, I went to this zemilyankas. There was not a door yet. Was just a rough sort of door. There was no-- so they put in a stove because of me, and they tried to find a door because of me. And I went to the other side of where the-- the-- the straw was, and brought my own straw and put it on the-- sort of the bank, which was from one end of the zemilyanka to the other. Everybody slept next-- next to each other. There was no divisions. And I put-- made myself a bed of straw-- talking about Jesus-- [LAUGHS] and a peasant's rag. And that was my bed for having my baby. No-- no curtains, no doors, no windows in cold zemilyankian winter. And I was covered in shingles on top of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, my husband stayed still on the other base, because he had to look after the provisions. And it took a few days. The baby didn't come before-- think about a week I was there, and didn't-- didn't get my real labor pains then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then all of a sudden I got my labor pains. And I was sent to the doctor, and the doctor wasn't there. The doctor was called out on an emergency. Somebody got wounded in the-- in the Russian partisans. All the time he was looking after me, when it came to the real thing he wasn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they send out somebody on a horse to fetch him. And this was-- this was starting during the night. He didn't come. And I was having terrific pain right through the night, labor pains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there wasn't-- only women and people came in, they brought little cinders-- wood. Used to go to the big fire to light them, because there wasn't any matches. They were wet. You couldn't find them. By the time they reached the zemilyankas they were-- so they come in-- they were out. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So almost in the darkness, and I had terrific pains. And nobody could help me. And I was feeling-- my husband wasn't with me, my doctor wasn't with me. And I thought I was going to die. I really-- I didn't care at this particular time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I didn't really want to bring a baby in this world, anyway. It just happened. I couldn't help it-- the circumstance, the nature, the fate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time something was going to happen, something else happened, you know? She was-- I tell my daughter, you were determined to come into this world. You've never seen such a resilient little baby that wanted to come out in this-- into this world. She said, I was always determined-- and she still is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was lying in my pain. The doctor's wife came. She knew something about this and some other lady who said she was a nurse-- but she wasn't a nurse. She pretended to be one. They were talking among themselves. They couldn't do anything for me. And this was already-- the night was out, and the doctor didn't come yet. Then it was getting worse and worse and worse and the doctor didn't come yet. And I was beginning to really-- to make funny noises because I felt it's the time. I need help. I was very young, and very small. And you know, I needed help-- some sort of help.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden a-- a lady from the next zemilyankas passes through, and she said, what's going on here? She saw there's some commotion. You know-- people took a rag and sort of hang it up so nobody can see me, you know, so I have a bit of privacy. And says, what's going on? And she said, well, a woman's having a baby and there's nobody to deliver it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, I can deliver a baby. And she walked in, she delivered the baby just like that-- in front of everybody. [LAUGHS] It took a few minutes, and she did it. And then after two hours, the doctor arrived. [LAUGHS] And he said he was so sorry, and he was so disappointed. I said, yeah. He loves to be present. And anyway it was such a big event. I mean, in a time when everyone was getting wiped out, a baby arrived in this world. And it was a little girl, of course. And so I was happy at this time, because we didn't need any brises, anything like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was it, you know? And I became a mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, when my husband was on this base, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody-- an airplane cruising, on exploration, a German, noticed the fire in the kitchen. There was a big kettle, sort of, where food was being for the whole triad cooked. And he noticed it and start-- with his machine gun, starting to shoot. And one bullet landed-- landed right in the-- in the kettle. So by this time, they decided that, even if this base isn't finished yet, they're going to transfer everything else. So that's when my husband came, and everybody else. But we-- this base wasn't far removed from this-- so I heard-- I heard the shooting. And everybody from the zemilyankas run away. So they said to me-- I was already tested. They said, leave the baby and go and save yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. I bought this-- it was one-- one day old, or one and a half days old. And I said, no, I'm not leaving. If-- if she goes, I go, you know? This baby, I brought in the world, I can't abandon her.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just, oh, that's only a baby. In those days a baby didn't have any-- a newborn baby, she'll die anyway. Can't bring a baby, can't survive. She'll die anyway. Just go and save yourself. I said, no. I've already been tested. So there you are. I was just sitting on my bed. I was sick anyway-- I couldn't get off the bed. But I suppose I could have run away. My feet were working. And I stayed, and nothing happened. They came back in the same night. Everybody, everybody came back to the zemilyankas, to this base. And this became our permanent place. And my husband was with me, and everybody else from the other base. And the other previous temporary base was abandoned and this became our permanent base.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it started to develop. You wouldn't believe what was going on. We almost stayed eight months in this base, like from the day like my daughter was born, like in November, end of November, till July '44, 10th of July. How many months is this? Seven, eight months?

speaker: interviewee
text: It developed into-- into a town, a shtetl. Had everything. You wouldn't believe what was-- we had tradesmen, we had builders, we had all sort of people from all different businesses that knew about everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So since they burnt out all the neighbor villages around the pushcha, in-- because they didn't want the partisan to have any resources. So still there were a lot of things abandoned like ovens, and stoves, and doors, and windows, and frames of all kind of-- tools, things for utility sort of use.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the tradesmen, one of the first things they built was a bakery. And you should've seen-- there was a special man who could build up such an oven with the help, of course, of-- had raw materials from the burned-out houses around all the villages. So we used to go and collect all those things. And the first came a bakery-- first we finished off all the zemilyankas. And then came the bakery-- we needed bread. And then came the-- the-- of course, the warehouse where all the provisions were kept. And my husband was in charge. And then came the-- of course the doctors' offices. And then came a sausage factory. We had real tradesmen-- you should have seen the hams and the bacons and the salamis. And some of it was kosher, because we had the kosher butchers there [LAUGHS] in [NON-ENGLISH], and one was a-- And we had a mill, which we got-- took it from somewhere, from abandoned flour mill. We had a tannery, made-- made leather.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a workshop where all the trades from-- from arms repair, to bootmakers, to-- to leatherworkers, to furriers, to whatever-- what trade you want? Watchmakers, everything. But nobody had watches. [LAUGHS] And then we had tailors, everything. And then we had-- what else did we have up there? We had a sausage factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a Turkish bath. There was a bathhouse in one of the villages. They removed it bit by bit and they installed it in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can go have a Turkish bath, and you can put your clothes-- this was a special in a-- in a sort of-- we called it [NON-ENGLISH], a room, a spot with the-- disinfected our clothes. Because we were all lice-ridden, you know? There's no escape from this. So when we went to the bath, everybody can go anytime they wanted. You put your clothes you came out clean-- they came out clean. And what else did we have? We had a hospital eventually. We had-- what else was there? A lot of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- like everything like in a town. All in eight month. And then we started building sort of custom-made zemilyankas. Like I was first in a zemilyankas with 30 people, with my baby, with my husband. And my husband worked at the other side, where the bakery was, and the sausage factory, was and the cattle. We had cows-- we were milking cows. And there was a special person we called Tevye the milker cow-- like in Tevye the Milkman from the And he was milking the cows and looking after them. There were horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what else was there? There was a school, children. You know, there was one lady-- she took the children. Primitive, but she taught them songs, she-- she taught them the alphabet, you know, because there was a lot of small children. And we put out concerts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came we had custom-built zemilyankas. Out of the big zemilyankas, we built smaller ones. So everybody picks somebody he wanted to be with like-- and they looked like houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So because my husband was in the warehouse, and next to it was the bakery, and next to us was the-- the sausage making and the cows. So they put us in a zemilyankas, custom made for me, my husband, and my baby. And then there was the one who was in charge of the bakery, and there was the one who was in charge of the sausage making. Three families.

speaker: interviewee
text: They build us a zemilyankas. And made a-- they build us a stove. I had hot water service there. They got a hold of a big kettle, they build it in, and something-- even there were that thing were you could bake.

speaker: interviewee
text: While the stove was burning, the water was sitting. And I had hot water for my baby's-- for the bath and for the washing. I never put the-- the nappies was from old torn up peasant sheets, but I never put anything on her that wasn't ironed. I had this one, this iron set with charcoal. You had to blow on it, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one of the things we used to borrow from each other. Never put on her something that wasn't ironed. She never had any-- any mark on her skin. And driving like-- like I don't know. But of course, I was breastfeeding all the time. The only marks she has from the mosquito bites, and there was lots and lots of mosquitoes in the forest, because there was swamps there. So that's how she-- she grew very healthy, no problems whatsoever-- the babies, no vaccinations, and no-- no formulas. [LAUGHS] And no proper clothes. Very-- very, very nicely. And that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This base which was a shtetl. And of course, it was the headquarters of the whole thing. And there was an infectious disease hospital in the other base and there was people--

speaker: interviewee
text: What we did, because we were a family at triad, and so didn't consider us as fighters, you know-- the Russians they made-- put pressure on us to be more-- more army-like than family-like. We put more-- more accent on surviving and rescuing, you know? And giving fugitives from the ghettos, from the [NON-ENGLISH] our protection, more than to fight the Germans. What could we do against the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we did had-- had so many fighters. We gave away 120 fighters who went to and formed a special group. And we had to have-- give concessions to the Russian partisan, because they were fighting. They expected us to fight. We were under their command.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we did instead, was giving them-- making-- providing them with sausages, with tradesmen, with bootmakers, with repair of the-- of the rifles and the arms, and baking for them bread. So every day, because we were in the zemilyankas from all the trees, took charge of the provisions, were in one zemilyankas. And most of the transactions went in our zemilyankas. They come from the other things-- they ordered so many loaves of bread, so many kilos of sausage, so many of ham. And for the traders, they're paid with other things, you know? They brought like the grain that we made flour out of it. They brought the-- they needed for the leather jackets, so we repaired their leather jackets or their fur coats, things like that. So we were sort of in this way, we helped them, and they helped us. So they tolerated us more, because we were providing them with all those things. So that's where we stayed. I had-- I had enough to eat. Did you stay there until liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, of course. And this was the last-- this was the permanent base. And actually things were getting around to trying to make another hunt-- big hunt, siege. But it never came to it, because they were-- the D-Day started, and the push from the east started, and from the west, and they were running. The Germans were running. Weren't just retreating-- they were running.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: We liberated in July. Actually as I said, the 9th. I say the 10th, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Book said the 9th. Some say 11. I say the 10th of July, '44. Before that, before the liberation, we still had a very sad event-- like the Germans were running, and they had maps. They had to run through the jungle, through the-- through the pushcha. Not far away was a canal. They had to get to the canal, they had to come through the things. They were running in disarray. They were not retreating, they're just running. So it's such a big push, that they were completely bewildered. And they were running through there, and of course they were caught by the partisans. But some-- some groups, they wanted to like-- they needed food, they needed to break through.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had arms. They had some of the best arms. They were coming right from the front. So one of these groups attacked us early in the morning because they were trying to get through and get some food. And so they attacked us first thing in the morning, very early. And we knew that a group's surrounding the forest, and we had to-- we kept watch for them. So we kept watch, and where are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was told to get out of the zemilyankas. Was a big thing, because by this time we were spread. I was close to the swamp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took my baby. And my husband was on the other side somewhere doing something. So I took my baby and then actually, my baby got-- got in the swamp. Only her little head was out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, all the partisans came from the forest and joined and pushed them back. They-- they were all killed, all the group. But in the meantime, 11 people-- on the eve of liberation, 11 people got killed.
